steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses: (1) ‘The sand at Spiaggia Rosa Beach is a more unusual shade’ and (2) ‘deposits of crushed coral and other organic matter lend the sand a unique pink hue.’ The word ‘though’ contrasts the unusual shade with ordinary sand, and it must attach to clause 1. Two independent clauses joined this way need a semicolon between them.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'shade, though,': two commas around ‘though’ leaves the clauses joined only by a comma — a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice B — 'shade, though': a single comma before ‘though’ still joins two independent clauses with only a comma. ✗
  • Choice C — 'shade; though': the semicolon is in the right spot but ‘though’ then opens clause 2, making it a subordinate fragment rather than a full independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'shade, though;': ‘though’ is set off by a comma and attaches to clause 1 as a trailing adverb; the semicolon then properly separates the two independent clauses. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
